perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall use its best efforts to safeguard against the unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information, and take security precautions at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential and proprietary information and materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall use commercially reasonable efforts to safeguard against the unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information, but will not be liable for breaches that occur despite such efforts.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'best efforts' to 'commercially reasonable efforts' and adding a liability waiver weakens the obligation to protect confidential information.  This introduces uncertainty and may conflict with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UTSA doesn't explicitly define 'best efforts', modifying the agreement to disclaim liability for breaches occurring despite 'commercially reasonable efforts' may be interpreted as a lower standard of care than required to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets, especially given the explicit acknowledgment of irreparable harm.  This weakens the protection afforded by trade secret law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by changing 'best efforts' to 'commercially reasonable efforts' and adding a liability waiver. This deviation from a higher standard of care potentially conflicts with the duty to maintain secrecy, especially considering the acknowledgment of potential irreparable harm as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By weakening the standard of care and disclaiming liability, the modified text arguably increases the risk of such a breach, thus contradicting the legal obligation to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it will not in any form export, reexport, resell, ship or divert or cause to be exported, reexported, resold, stripped or diverted, directly or indirectly, any product or technical data to any country for which the United States Government or any agency thereof at the time of export or reexport requires an export license or other government approval without first obtaining such approval.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it will not directly export, reexport, resell, ship or divert any product or technical data to countries sanctioned by the United States Government without obtaining prior approval from FormFactor.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the restriction to 'direct' actions and removing the reference to 'any agency thereof' and 'other government approval' narrows the scope of export control compliance. It also places the onus of approval on FormFactor, instead of the appropriate government agency.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR broadly prohibits exports requiring a license without such a license.  By limiting the restriction to 'direct' exports and removing the explicit requirement for obtaining 'other government approval' for activities that would normally require it, the modified text allows the recipient to bypass export control requirements that are mandated under federal law, thereby contradicting 15 CFR § 736.2.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by narrowing the scope of export control compliance, deviating from the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The original text prevents any export without proper approval. The changed text limits the restriction to 'direct' actions and removes the explicit requirement for obtaining 'other government approval,' potentially allowing the recipient to bypass export control requirements mandated under federal law. As supported by Law Explanation, this contradicts 15 CFR § 736.2, because EAR broadly prohibits exports requiring a license without such a license. The Bureau of Industry and Security snippet says that the EAR can be found under 15 CFR 730-774.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient hereby acknowledges and agrees that unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information Would cause serious and irreparable harm and significant injury to FormFactor that may be difficult or impossible to ascertain. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that FormFactor will have, in addition to all outer remedies at law or in equity, the right to seek and obtain immediate injunctive relief for the actual or threatened unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information might cause harm to FormFactor. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that FormFactor may seek remedies at law, but not injunctive relief, for unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the acknowledgement of 'serious and irreparable harm' and preventing 'injunctive relief' significantly limits FormFactor's legal recourse in case of a breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under California's version of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, injunctive relief is a primary remedy for misappropriation of trade secrets when there is a threat of continued misappropriation or actual misappropriation has occurred.  By contractually preventing FormFactor from seeking injunctive relief, the modified agreement directly contradicts the remedies available under state law for trade secret violations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California law regarding trade secret misappropriation. The original text acknowledges 'serious and irreparable harm' and allows FormFactor to seek injunctive relief. The changed text removes this acknowledgment and explicitly prevents FormFactor from seeking injunctive relief. California Civil Code Section 3426.2(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that 'Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined.' By preventing injunctive relief, the changed contract directly contradicts the available remedies under California law, thus creating a legal contradiction as FormFactor is waiving its right to injunctive relief, a remedy explicitly provided by state law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to its conflict of laws principles. The parties hereby agree to submit themselves to the jurisdiction of the federal and state courts within Santa Clara County, California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict of laws principles. The parties agree to resolve disputes through binding arbitration in New York City.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware and mandating arbitration in New York alters the legal framework and venue for dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally supports arbitration agreements, the choice of law provision and the mandatory arbitration clause can be problematic if they effectively waive a party's substantive rights under a different state's laws (California in the original agreement), particularly relating to injunctive relief for trade secret violations.  The FAA allows challenges to arbitration agreements based on general contract defenses. If the change to Delaware law and mandatory arbitration in New York is deemed to unfairly prejudice FormFactor's ability to protect its trade secrets under California law (where FormFactor is located), it could be argued that the agreement is unconscionable and unenforceable under the FAA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by altering the governing law from California to Delaware and mandating arbitration in New York City. This shift could potentially undermine the substantive rights that a party, specifically FormFactor, would have under California law, particularly concerning trade secret protection. The Law Explanation states that if the change to Delaware law and mandatory arbitration in New York is deemed to unfairly prejudice FormFactor's ability to protect its trade secrets under California law, it could be argued that the agreement is unconscionable and unenforceable under the FAA. Scraped Law Snippet 2, derived from 9 U.S. Code § 2, affirms the validity and enforceability of arbitration agreements, but crucially includes the clause 'save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract.' This clause provides an avenue to challenge the arbitration agreement if the alteration of governing law and venue is deemed to unfairly prejudice a party's rights under the original jurisdiction (California).
